category_summary:
The AI community was dominated by dueling enterprise agent platform launches. **OpenAI** [unveiled workspace agents](/?date=2026-04-23&category=social#item-3457bb28d6d0) built on **Codex**, with **Greg Brockman** detailing cloud-hosted agents connected to **Slack** and recurring tasks, while **Sam Altman** [endorsed the product](/?date=2026-04-23&category=social#item-af404e7251d3) to massive engagement. Simultaneously, **Google DeepMind** [launched the **Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform**](/?date=2026-04-23&category=social#item-6b6727aa95b8) with **Google Cloud**, signaling agentic AI as the primary enterprise battleground.

- **Ethan Mollick** [delivered the day's sharpest insight](/?date=2026-04-23&category=social#item-9250f039f5f1): every system implicitly regulated by human effort—recommendation letters, lawsuits, government filings—will break as AI removes those effort constraints
- **Aravind Srinivas** [revealed **Perplexity** post-trained](/?date=2026-04-23&category=social#item-6ea0be5d5618) a model on **Qwen** achieving Pareto-optimal accuracy-cost, already serving production traffic and outperforming **GPT** and **Sonnet** on efficiency
- **Jeremy Howard** [sharply criticized **Anthropic**](/?date=2026-04-23&category=social#item-904eb3ff9da7) for quietly removing **Claude Code** mentions from Pro plan documentation, calling it a collapse of integrity
- **Sony AI** [published a **Nature** paper](/?date=2026-04-23&category=social#item-b23e54f8b507) on the first autonomous robot to beat elite humans at competitive table tennis
- A developer [reverse-engineered **Claude Mythos**](/?date=2026-04-23&category=social#item-df85886591cf), revealing a novel adaptive-depth architecture where a single block loops up to 16 times per forward pass
- **NVIDIA** and **Google Cloud** [expanded their partnership](/?date=2026-04-23&category=social#item-27d5d1b98826) around **Vera Rubin** A5X instances scaling toward ~1M GPUs
category_summary_html:
The AI community was dominated by dueling enterprise agent platform launches. OpenAI unveiled workspace agents built on Codex, with Greg Brockman detailing cloud-hosted agents connected to Slack and recurring tasks, while Sam Altman endorsed the product to massive engagement. Simultaneously, Google DeepMind launched the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform with Google Cloud, signaling agentic AI as the primary enterprise battleground.

  • Ethan Mollick delivered the day's sharpest insight: every system implicitly regulated by human effort—recommendation letters, lawsuits, government filings—will break as AI removes those effort constraints
  • Aravind Srinivas revealed Perplexity post-trained a model on Qwen achieving Pareto-optimal accuracy-cost, already serving production traffic and outperforming GPT and Sonnet on efficiency
  • Jeremy Howard sharply criticized Anthropic for quietly removing Claude Code mentions from Pro plan documentation, calling it a collapse of integrity
  • Sony AI published a Nature paper on the first autonomous robot to beat elite humans at competitive table tennis
  • A developer reverse-engineered Claude Mythos, revealing a novel adaptive-depth architecture where a single block loops up to 16 times per forward pass
  • NVIDIA and Google Cloud expanded their partnership around Vera Rubin A5X instances scaling toward ~1M GPUs

items.title:
We’ve post trained a model on top of Qwen that achieves Pareto optimality on accuracy-cost curves. 
...
items.content:
We’ve post trained a model on top of Qwen that achieves Pareto optimality on accuracy-cost curves. 

Unlike our previous post trained models, this model has been trained to be good at search and tool calls simultaneously, allowing us to unify the tool call router and summarization together in one model. 

The resulting model performs better than GPT and Sonnet in terms of cost efficiency to serve daily Perplexity queries in production. The production model runs on our own inference platform.

We’re already serving a significant chunk of our daily traffic with this model and intend to have it serve all of default traffic pretty soon. 

More research to follow soon on models we’re training and deploying for Comet and Computer.

items.summary: Arav Srinivas (Perplexity CEO) announces they've post-trained a model on Qwen that achieves Pareto-optimal accuracy-cost curves, unifying tool-call routing and summarization. It outperforms GPT and Sonnet in cost efficiency and is already serving significant production traffic.
items.reasoning: Major announcement from Perplexity CEO about a custom post-trained model already in production. High engagement (444 likes, 69K views). Reveals Perplexity's strategy of building on open-source Qwen rather than relying on frontier API models. Mentions upcoming research for Comet and Computer products. Significant industry signal.

Unlike our previous post trained models, this model has been trained to be good at search and tool calls simultaneously, allowing us to unify the tool call router and summarization together in one model.

The resulting model performs better than GPT and Sonnet in terms of cost efficiency to serve daily Perplexity queries in production. The production model runs on our own inference platform.

items.title: Sony AI just published the first autonomous robot to beat elite humans at a competitive physical spo...
items.content:
Sony AI just published the first autonomous robot to beat elite humans at a competitive physical sport.

Its name is Ace. The sport is table tennis. The paper dropped in Nature today.

9 cameras triangulate the ball in 3D. Three systems zoom in on the ball's logo mid-flight to read its spin axis. 

End-to-end reaction time is around 20 milliseconds, roughly 10x faster than a human.

It learned by playing itself for 3,000 hours in simulation. No human demonstrations.

In April 2025, Ace beat 3 of 5 elite players and lost to both pros. By December, it had beaten a pro.

At one point, Ace returned an impossible backspin shot. Kinjiro Nakamura, a 1992 Barcelona Olympian, watched from the side:

"No one else would have been able to do that. I didn't think it was possible. But the fact that it was possible means there is a possibility that a human could do it too."

items.content:
This wasn't the case with previous image generators, but the LLM you select has a huge effect on GPT-imagegen-2  output. GPT-5.4 Thinking and GPT-5.4 Pro will produce much better images, especially for complex things.

This is, of course, not intuitive or explained anywhere.

GPT-5.4 Thinking and GPT-5.4 Pro produce much better images, especially for complex tasks. Notes this isn't intuitive or documented.

items.title:
A developer just reverse engineered Claude Mythos and open sourced it. 

It's called OpenMythos and ...
items.content:
A developer just reverse engineered Claude Mythos and open sourced it. 

It's called OpenMythos and implemented in PyTorch.

The core idea: instead of stacking hundreds of unique layers, a single block runs up to 16 times per forward pass.

Same weights, more loops, deeper thinking.

A 770M parameter version matches a 1.3B standard transformer in quality.

Reasoning happens silently in continuous latent space, with no chain-of-thought tokens emitted between steps.

Each iteration activates a different subset of experts, so every pass is genuinely distinct computation.

What this unlocks:

> Reasoning depth scales at inference
> Memory footprint stays flat
> Hard tokens get more compute
> Easy tokens halt early
> Stability guaranteed by construction

The relevant axis is no longer parameter count at training.

It's loop depth at inference.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Developer reverse-engineered Claude Mythos and open-sourced it as 'OpenMythos' in PyTorch. Key architecture: single block runs up to 16 times per forward pass (looped transformer). 770M params matches 1.3B standard transformer. Reasoning in latent space without CoT tokens. Adaptive compute with early halting.
items.reasoning: Highly significant technical content about Claude Mythos architecture reverse-engineering. Reveals looped transformer design with adaptive compute depth, latent-space reasoning, and MoE activation per loop. The claim that 'the relevant axis is loop depth at inference, not parameter count' is a potentially important architectural insight. Good engagement (19K views).

items.title: Judging AGI by how well it can mimic us is a category error, because mimicry isn't intelligence and ...
items.content: Judging AGI by how well it can mimic us is a category error, because mimicry isn't intelligence and isn't general. We should judge AGI by how well it learns to do things we didn't teach it (including things we don't know how to do ourselves).
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: François Chollet argues that judging AGI by mimicry is a category error. AGI should be judged by ability to learn things not taught, including things humans can't do.
items.reasoning: Deeply substantive take on AGI evaluation from Keras creator and ARC benchmark author. Very high engagement (587 likes, 33K views). Directly relevant to ongoing AGI definition debates.

items.title:
Researchers reverse-engineered Claude Code. Only 1.6% is actually AI.

The other 98.4% is infrastruc...
items.content:
Researchers reverse-engineered Claude Code. Only 1.6% is actually AI.

The other 98.4% is infrastructure around the model.

Permission gates, tool routing, context compaction, session recovery. 

The model just reasons. Everything else runs the show.

The core loop is a plain while-true. 

Call the model, run tools, repeat.

The real engineering lives in the systems wrapping that loop:

> Seven-mode permission system
> Five-layer context compaction pipeline
> Four extension mechanisms by cost
> Subagents isolated in sidechain files

Users approve 93% of prompts anyway, so automation replaces endless warnings.

Agent teams still burn roughly 7x the tokens of a single session.

The bet is clear. As frontier models converge on coding skill, the harness becomes the real differentiator.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Analysis of Claude Code's architecture: only 1.6% is AI model calls, 98.4% is infrastructure (permission gates, tool routing, context compaction, session recovery). Seven-mode permission system, five-layer context compaction, subagents in sidechain files. Agent teams use ~7x tokens of single sessions.
items.reasoning: Insightful reverse-engineering of Claude Code's architecture revealing that agent infrastructure vastly outweighs the AI component. The specific numbers (1.6% AI, 7x token overhead for teams) and architectural details are valuable. Low engagement suggests early post, but content quality is high.

items.title:
Been hearing wild stuff from folks inside big companies lately.

Promotions, firings, and perf revie...
items.content:
Been hearing wild stuff from folks inside big companies lately.

Promotions, firings, and perf reviews are getting decided by tokens consumed and skills/MCPs connected. That’s the metric. That’s how they’re deciding who’s “good at AI.”

It gets worse. People are literally running loops to burn tokens and look productive. Doing nothing, racking up “usage,” getting rewarded for it.

Meanwhile the person actually shipping with 2 skills and 50M tokens looks like a laggard next to the one who burned a billion tokens producing nothing.

These companies are walking into a death spiral and don’t see it.

The funniest part? Measuring actual output is easier than ever. You have AI. Use it.

In 18 months the same execs will announce “AI didn’t deliver ROI” and pull the budget. AI will have worked fine. They just measured the wrong fucking thing and torched millions rewarding theater over output.

Every company should be pushing AI hard. But this is how you guarantee it fails.

items.title:
MIT researchers just replicated human muscles with AI-controlled fibers.

Inside each fiber is a sea...
items.content:
MIT researchers just replicated human muscles with AI-controlled fibers.

Inside each fiber is a sealed tube of electrically charged liquid and a tiny electric pump.

When the pump activates, one side contracts while the other relaxes, just as your biceps and triceps do when you bend your arm.

How it works:

> The pump injects electrical charge into the fluid
> This creates ions that drag the liquid along with them
> No motors, no external pumps, completely silent

Because they're fibers, they bundle together just like real muscles, scaling up force by adding more strands.

In demos, these fibers were strong enough to bend a robotic arm and curl a dumbbell... but gentle enough to shake someone's hand.

From prosthetics to exoskeletons to industrial robots, this is what happens when engineers stop building around motors and start building around biology.
items.author: @rowancheung
items.summary: MIT researchers created AI-controlled fibers replicating human muscles using electrically charged liquid and tiny pumps. Silent, no motors, scalable by bundling. Demonstrated bending robotic arms and curling dumbbells while gentle enough for handshakes.
items.reasoning: Fascinating robotics/materials science breakthrough from MIT with very high engagement (104K views, 1K likes). Bio-inspired actuators without motors could be significant for soft robotics. Though the poster is a newsletter account, the underlying research is compelling.

items.title: LiteParse, our OSS document parser, is really good at parsing complex PDF layouts, text, and tables ...
items.content:
LiteParse, our OSS document parser, is really good at parsing complex PDF layouts, text, and tables into a clean spatial grid. 

The best part is it doesn't use VLMs or any ML models at all. It's entirely heuristics based and super fast ⚡️ 

The secret lies in our sophisticated grid projection algorithm. This blog post by @LoganMarkewich gives a comprehensive walkthrough on how it works: 
1️⃣ Sort lines based on similar Y coordinates
2️⃣ Extract left, right, and center anchors
3️⃣ Classify every text item into one of these anchors
4️⃣ Project every text item into a grid column (the exception is any paragraph of flowing text, which is rendered separately) 
5️⃣ For any item projected into a grid column, that item is the forward anchor for all subsequent text items with the same anchor 
6️⃣ Postprocess the final outputs to remove extraneous spaces and margins

As an example, take a look at the results below. You can see text in the left column, with a nicely overlaid table on the right.

LiteParse is fully free and open-source, you can use it today! Either directly through the CLI or integrated into your coding agent. 

Blog: https://t.co/OnMZtLTzGT

LiteParse repo: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex CEO) introduces LiteParse, an open-source document parser that handles complex PDF layouts using purely heuristic-based algorithms (no VLMs or ML). Details sophisticated grid projection algorithm for parsing text, tables, and complex layouts.
items.reasoning: Significant open-source tool from LlamaIndex. Notable that it uses zero ML models - purely heuristic-based approach for document parsing. Strong engagement (176 likes, 19K views). Technical depth with algorithm walkthrough. Practical utility for RAG pipelines.

Workspace agents can work across tools—pulling context from docs, email, chats, code, and systems, and taking approved actions like updating @Linear issues, creating docs, or sending messages.

In @SlackHQ, agents can jump into a thread, understand what’s needed, pull the right context, help resolve the issue, and update the right systems.

They can also keep going in the background or on a schedule, while you’re away.

items.title:
Finally, a personal note on how this paper was made.

I started this as a hobby project exactly two ...
items.content:
Finally, a personal note on how this paper was made.

I started this as a hobby project exactly two months ago, building the entire system with Claude Code. The process reminded me of when I was a professor working with my grad students — brainstorming ideas, staring at plots, proposing experiments — but never looking at their code.

Claude Code is the ideal collaborator for this style of research. And this is what enabled my first single-author paper ever!

items.summary: Kevin Murphy shares that he wrote his first single-author paper using Claude Code as collaborator, comparing the experience to working with grad students - brainstorming ideas and staring at plots but never looking at code.
items.reasoning: Compelling personal testimony from a senior ML researcher about AI-assisted research workflows. The 'first single-author paper ever' angle enabled by Claude Code is a noteworthy signal about AI transforming academic research.

In the study, we find that respondents in both the highest- and the lowest-paid occupations report the largest productivity gains from AI.

But people with the largest speedups also had the greatest concern about job displacement.

items.title: There seems to be some excitement around “ChatGPT’s powerful new image engine”, but as ever, its fun...
items.content:
There seems to be some excitement around “ChatGPT’s powerful new image engine”, but as ever, its functional understanding of the world seems limited. 

I first learned about the new system when some some smart aleck on X sent me an example of the new system trying to label a bike (an example I have considered before), with the caption “Uh oh”, apparently believing that my longstanding challenges to image generation had been solved. 
￼
It does look impressive on first inspection, better than some examples I showed here before. 

But if you look closely, there are several errors, and those errors are revealing. For example, the rear center-pull (?) brake was mislabeled as the seat stay, and the big gear on the back was mislabeled as the rear brake. There was a label for a spoke that is pointing to blank space.

In many modern bikes, of course, a rear brake can be found back there, but not in this diagram. Instead this system has combined a typical position for a modern disc brake system with a diagram of an older (though still in use) caliper (or similar) system. The system doesn’t actually understand how the various parts function.

And of course there are literally hundreds of labeled bikes on the internet as a quick Google Image Search would reveal. (Which is why my usual test here has been a tandem bike, to make things a little more challenging.)

To up the degree of difficulty, I asked ChatGPT to “please draw a taller than average tandem bike, and include a bike rack and panniers”, which is not something you could readily find on the internet, and not something I used here before, and got the bike-wreck of a picture I include below.

Bike nuts would have a field day finding problems with it. (Feel free to drop your favorite error in the comments). 

Suffice to say that most people don’t stuff their rear derailleur in the back wheel. And I don’t even know what to say about that “rear brake lever”, or the saddle-shaped rear handlebar, let alone the “rear brake” that is somehow part of the rear rack. 

As in the first example, the lack of functional understanding is manifest.

Of course, to be fair, the average human couldn’t complete this task, either. 

But anybody knowledgeable about bikes (racers, mechanics, designers etc) would immediately see numerous problems. 

And honestly is anybody tall enough to ride in the front?
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Gary Marcus provides a detailed analysis of AI image generation limitations using bicycle diagrams. Shows that while AI has improved at labeling common objects, it still fails at functional understanding - confusing brake types, mislabeling parts, and failing when pushed beyond web-searchable examples like a tall tandem bike with panniers.
items.reasoning: Most substantive post in Marcus's bike thread. Detailed technical critique with specific examples demonstrating the difference between pattern matching and functional understanding. Good engagement (68 likes, 9K views). Methodologically sound testing approach.
items.summary_html: Gary Marcus provides a detailed analysis of AI image generation limitations using bicycle diagrams. Shows that while AI has improved at labeling common objects, it still fails at functional understanding - confusing brake types, mislabeling parts, and failing when pushed beyond web-searchable examples like a tall tandem bike with panniers.

items.title: Fascinating how AI is getting better at diagrams like these (at least for ones that you could easily...
items.content:
Fascinating how AI is getting better at diagrams like these (at least for ones that you could easily find on web search) but still making some pretty wacky errors  — like confusing where the rear brake is* — that no knowledgeable human would make.

What this reflects is an ongoing lack of *functional* understanding of parts.**

—-

*Look closely for other errors, like the labeling of an empty space as a spoke.

**For extra challenge, try similar experiments for things that don’t have lots of extant label examples already on the web.

items.summary: Marcus observes AI is improving at diagram labeling for web-searchable examples but still makes errors no knowledgeable human would, due to lack of functional understanding of parts. Suggests testing with examples that don't have many existing labeled versions online.
items.reasoning: Well-articulated main thesis post. Very high views (63K) and good engagement (173 likes). Provides a useful framework for evaluating AI visual understanding: memorization vs. functional understanding.

Fascinating how AI is getting better at diagrams like these (at least for ones that you could easily find on web search) but still making some pretty wacky errors — like confusing where the rear brake is* — that no knowledgeable human would make.

What this reflects is an ongoing lack of *functional* understanding of parts.**

—-

*Look closely for other errors, like the labeling of an empty space as a spoke.

**For extra challenge, try similar experiments for things that don’t have lots of extant label examples already on the web.

items.title: For real agentic workloads (North), short-context calibration wasn't enough. We calibrated AWQ on lo...
items.content: For real agentic workloads (North), short-context calibration wasn't enough. We calibrated AWQ on long internal agentic traces (up to 64k tokens) and added token masking in llm-compressor to exclude repetitive chat templates/tool descriptions from calibration stats. Plus QAD (quant-aware distillation) to close the last gap — matching the quality of our BF16 MoE model with W4A8.
items.author: @cohere
items.summary: Cohere describes calibrating AWQ on long agentic traces (up to 64k tokens) with token masking and quant-aware distillation for their North agentic workloads, matching BF16 MoE quality with W4A8.
items.reasoning: Technically detailed insight into production quantization for agentic workloads. The long-context calibration approach for agentic traces is a practical contribution.
items.summary_html: Cohere describes calibrating AWQ on long agentic traces (up to 64k tokens) with token masking and quant-aware distillation for their North agentic workloads, matching BF16 MoE quality with W4A8.
items.content_html: For real agentic workloads (North), short-context calibration wasn't enough. We calibrated AWQ on long internal agentic traces (up to 64k tokens) and added token masking in llm-compressor to exclude repetitive chat templates/tool descriptions from calibration stats. Plus QAD (quant-aware distillation) to close the last gap — matching the quality of our BF16 MoE model with W4A8.

The @NVIDIA Jetson team published a t...
items.content:
🎉 Great to see vLLM powering Google Gemma 4 on NVIDIA Jetson.

The @NVIDIA Jetson team published a tutorial covering the full Gemma 4 family, from E2B / E4B (audio + image + text) to 26B-A4B MoE and 31B dense. vLLM ships native tool calling and reasoning parser support for Gemma 4.

Thanks to the @NVIDIARobotics team!
Learn more 👇

items.summary: vLLM now powers Google Gemma 4 on NVIDIA Jetson, with a tutorial covering the full Gemma 4 family including audio+image+text models, MoE variants, and dense models. Includes native tool calling and reasoning parser support.

items.title:
Open-source always finds a way!

This is an open-source, local-first memory layer for LLM agents.

I...
items.content:
Open-source always finds a way!

This is an open-source, local-first memory layer for LLM agents.

It's for Mac users.

This captures what you are doing and writes it in Markdown files.

For example, it writes down the things you are building, your prompts, the tools you use, and whatever happens on your computer.

Then, you can share that memory layer with any agent.

items.title: 🔧 The tricky part: naïvely casting BF16 group scales to FP8 dropped the quality. Our fix: quantize s...
items.content: 🔧 The tricky part: naïvely casting BF16 group scales to FP8 dropped the quality. Our fix: quantize scales per-channel (outer vector scaling) + rescale by 1/8 to avoid FP8 clipping. Result: >99.5% of W4A16 accuracy recovered on Command A & Cohere MoE. Paired with a CUTLASS LUT-based dequant path, we get near-peak FP8 throughput.
items.author: @cohere
items.summary: Cohere details solving FP8 group scale quantization quality drop through per-channel outer vector scaling and 1/8 rescaling, achieving >99.5% W4A16 accuracy recovery with CUTLASS LUT-based dequant.
items.reasoning: Deep technical detail on production quantization techniques. Specific and novel approach to FP8 scale quantization. Moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Cohere details solving FP8 group scale quantization quality drop through per-channel outer vector scaling and 1/8 rescaling, achieving >99.5% W4A16 accuracy recovery with CUTLASS LUT-based dequant.
items.content_html: 🔧 The tricky part: naïvely casting BF16 group scales to FP8 dropped the quality. Our fix: quantize scales per-channel (outer vector scaling) + rescale by 1/8 to avoid FP8 clipping. Result: >99.5% of W4A16 accuracy recovered on Command A & Cohere MoE. Paired with a CUTLASS LUT-based dequant path, we get near-peak FP8 throughput.

items.title:
been using claude design and loving it

it's basically claude code under the hood but with a very op...
items.content:
been using claude design and loving it

it's basically claude code under the hood but with a very opinionated ontology for input and output

- the input ontology is logo, colors, fonts, etc.
- the output ontology is slides, decks, business cards as html (with further ontology: headers, tables, etc.)

the input ontology is your brand guidelines, which is also the "shared context" for whenever you trigger claude design

i can drop in a blog post, and say "turn it into slides" and the deck it creates follows the brand guideline

you can export this to ppt/pdf, which isn't always perfect (also, weirdly doesn't work if you hit your claude design limit - it currently has a different limit from your claude plan)

definitely worth playing around with, if not to create your own designs, but at least to look at these design patterns - there are other places you can apply this
items.author: @yoheinakajima
items.summary: Yohei Nakajima reviews Claude Design, describing it as Claude Code with opinionated input/output ontologies for design. Input = brand guidelines (logo, colors, fonts), output = slides, decks, business cards as HTML. Can convert blog posts to branded slide decks.
items.reasoning: Useful first-hand review of Claude Design from a credible AI builder. Interesting architectural insight about how it uses brand guidelines as shared context. Practical limitations noted (export issues, separate rate limits).
items.summary_html: Yohei Nakajima reviews Claude Design, describing it as Claude Code with opinionated input/output ontologies for design. Input = brand guidelines (logo, colors, fonts), output = slides, decks, business cards as HTML. Can convert blog posts to branded slide decks.
items.content_html:
been using claude design and loving it

it's basically claude code under the hood but with a very opinionated ontology for input and output

  • the input ontology is logo, colors, fonts, etc.
  • the output ontology is slides, decks, business cards as html (with further ontology: headers, tables, etc.)

the input ontology is your brand guidelines, which is also the "shared context" for whenever you trigger claude design

i can drop in a blog post, and say "turn it into slides" and the deck it creates follows the brand guideline

you can export this to ppt/pdf, which isn't always perfect (also, weirdly doesn't work if you hit your claude design limit - it currently has a different limit from your claude plan)

definitely worth playing around with, if not to create your own designs, but at least to look at these design patterns - there are other places you can apply this

items.title: It takes less than 10 minutes to make an app out of X over on @Pokee_AI. Which includes the X API fo...
items.content:
It takes less than 10 minutes to make an app out of X over on @Pokee_AI. Which includes the X API for free in its low token costs.

I talk to @AndrewWarner about why this is so powerful. 

And it is cheaper now to do the same with your OpenClaw or Hermes or Manus or almost any AI agentic system. 

It's how I built https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ.

Why is this a big deal? Well, EVERYONE can now build software. I can show you how to do this in a few minutes. I'm not a programmer, and I built this system just by talking to an AI about what I wanted. 

"Build me a website of all the important news from the AI community." 

Yeah, it took me two months to really teach my agents how to look at the news and what I wanted, but it really is that easy to build software now.

Everyone can build an app for their communities and teach it what to find that's valuable here on X.

items.title: @OhSweeetNothing @BrianRoemmele The problem with the algorithm (hi Grok) is that it has been told to...
items.content:
@OhSweeetNothing @BrianRoemmele The problem with the algorithm (hi Grok) is that it has been told to serve the product needs of X. Find quick, easily read, posts. 

I actually like the feed and the new AI list that got turned on yesterday for iPhone users. Both are pretty good.

But it does ignore certain kinds of longer thinking, especially from people who have years of experience and insights like you and me. 

It used to be that we got reach because of the advantages of being here for years. Big followings, and people saw us as more insightful than others because of who we talk to, and all that. 

Now the graph has been flattened. It no longer matters how many followers you have. Most don't see our posts on a regular basis. 

Why not? 

Well, X has changed. It's gotten way way busier, for one. On just my AI lists there are 40,000 posts a day coursing through X's veins. That's a huge shift up from a few years ago. The AI community has gotten bigger and is more prolific.

Also, @nikitabier and Elon are trying to appeal to new users to win back people who have left for Threads, Bluesky, TikTok, and other places. 

That means not just focusing on those with big accounts, but spreading the love over a much bigger, more diverse set, of people.

You see it, people who don't get hits complain and leave. 

And if a new user comes here and only sees shit content they leave and go somewhere else. 

The problem with X is "what does it want to be when it grows up?"

Reddit is a better place to discuss things. TikTok or Instagram is a better place to get entertained. LinkedIn is a better place for professional networking. Bluesky is a better place for finding a tribe. And I could go on.

I was trying to make X a better place for AI community with my lists and my effort over at https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ and I think the new "AI" list on iPhones is a good step along those lines. It has a good diversity and not much noise yet. 

But this could be a lot better. X audio spaces suck in a number of different ways. They could really be improved to make classes and discussions a lot more interesting, but they have calcified into little tribal conversations. 

And people like you and me are frustrated by seeing how hard it is to get people to 1. See us. 2. Pay attention. 

Where is it all going? I have no idea, but I like that Nikita is trying things to improve the service for most people. The feed has gotten better. But is that enough? 

I don't think so. And, yes, longer posts and more intellectual thinking has gotten devalued, and longer videos don't fit in here well.

Most people are hyper busy and just looking to see the news quickly and dive out. They probably only have time to see fewer than 100 posts out of the 40,000 (and that's just in the AI world, there are hundreds of millions of posts in many different micro communities here). 

One last thing: AI has really devalued the long post too. A lot of the long posts I see are clearly written by AI, or greatly influenced by it. 

We should do a show on this. It's a tough one to figure out what feedback to give @ElonMusk and @nikitabier because media itself is changing so deeply due to AI and it's changing all of us in ways we haven't really wrapped our heads around.

And when Optimus and brain/computer interfaces enter our lives this all will radically change again. 

That said, I'm not going anywhere. I just don't like the other services. I tried to like them, but they just aren't for me. 

Even if it's just the two of us talking with each other I'm not leaving. :-)

items.title:
One of the biggest problems with real-time streaming is the need to normalize text.

For example, if...
items.content:
One of the biggest problems with real-time streaming is the need to normalize text.

For example, if you want to say "Dr. Smith makes $350/hr", you need to normalize the text to something like "Doctor Smith makes three hundred fifty dollars per hour."

We use normalization pipelines for that.

They work, but they introduce a lot of problems:

• Added latency on every single request
• Another moving part to maintain and monitor
• Brittle rules that break on edge cases
• Harder to debug when something sounds wrong

Async just published the first open benchmark measuring exactly this, and the differences across providers are significant.

When you are streaming audio in real time, you don't have time for preprocessing. Instead, you want the model to handle the normalization in-line, instantly.

This model performs in-line normalization during streaming, with no preprocessing and no text rewriting.

Look at the attached benchmark results. You can see how much difference there is across providers.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino discusses a key challenge in real-time TTS streaming: text normalization (e.g., converting '$350/hr' to spoken form). Highlights Async's first open benchmark for this, showing a model that handles in-line normalization during streaming without preprocessing.
items.reasoning: Addresses important technical challenge in TTS streaming. First open benchmark for text normalization in streaming context. Practical implications for voice AI applications. Good engagement.
items.summary_html: Svpino discusses a key challenge in real-time TTS streaming: text normalization (e.g., converting '$350/hr' to spoken form). Highlights Async's first open benchmark for this, showing a model that handles in-line normalization during streaming without preprocessing.

items.title:
Instead of copying and pasting errors into Claude Code, do this:

$ cat error.log | claude "fix this...
items.content:
Instead of copying and pasting errors into Claude Code, do this:

$ cat error.log | claude "fix this"

Easier, and usually gives Claude way more context than a simple copy/paste.

(Also, pretty simple to add this to your CI/CD so it automatically fixes things or tells you what to do.)

items.title: The result holds up under scrutiny. Using ANOVA + paired analysis + bootstrap p-values to control fo...
items.content: The result holds up under scrutiny. Using ANOVA + paired analysis + bootstrap p-values to control for question difficulty (which accounts for 62% of Brier variance), BLF beats every comparator at p<0.01 or better. We're also the only method that significantly beats the crowd on market questions — a bar nothing else clears.

items.title: ✨ I made a little demo of #vibejam sponsor @tripoai and how you can use it to generate assets for yo...
items.content:
✨ I made a little demo of #vibejam sponsor @tripoai and how you can use it to generate assets for your games

In this case I'm generating an apartment flat that's half destroyed and open for my drone sim to fly around, take cover and shoot at enemies from

First I write the prompt and select Nano Banana to generate an image first, I get a few different ones, and I select the one I like

Then that one is converted into a 3d object called a Smart Mesh

Smart Mesh is different than regular image-to-3d models because usually you get issues with uneven polygon distribution, too dense geometry and poor edge flow, so while you think you save time with generating it with AI you end up doing lots of editing after to fix the topology which kinda defeats the point

Smart Mesh doesn't have any of this, it's fast and clean low-poly models you can drop into your game

Anyway, after generating the model I can then see it in 3d, and if I don't like it, regenerate it, or if I like it start texturing it

Once that's done, I export it as a GLB, FBX or OBJ and drop it in (usually I use GLB as ThreeJS seems to prefer that), in my case I just drop the URL into Cursor and let it figure out the placement of the asset into my game!
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio demos Tripo AI's Smart Mesh feature for generating 3D game assets from text/image prompts. Highlights that Smart Mesh produces clean low-poly models without topology issues, exportable as GLB/FBX/OBJ for direct use in games via Cursor.
items.reasoning: Practical demo of AI-powered 3D asset generation workflow for game dev (vibejam context). Sponsored content but informative. Decent engagement at 39K views.
items.summary_html: Levelsio demos Tripo AI's Smart Mesh feature for generating 3D game assets from text/image prompts. Highlights that Smart Mesh produces clean low-poly models without topology issues, exportable as GLB/FBX/OBJ for direct use in games via Cursor.
items.content_html:
✨ I made a little demo of #vibejam sponsor @tripoai and how you can use it to generate assets for your games

In this case I'm generating an apartment flat that's half destroyed and open for my drone sim to fly around, take cover and shoot at enemies from

First I write the prompt and select Nano Banana to generate an image first, I get a few different ones, and I select the one I like

Then that one is converted into a 3d object called a Smart Mesh

Smart Mesh is different than regular image-to-3d models because usually you get issues with uneven polygon distribution, too dense geometry and poor edge flow, so while you think you save time with generating it with AI you end up doing lots of editing after to fix the topology which kinda defeats the point

Smart Mesh doesn't have any of this, it's fast and clean low-poly models you can drop into your game

Anyway, after generating the model I can then see it in 3d, and if I don't like it, regenerate it, or if I like it start texturing it

Once that's done, I export it as a GLB, FBX or OBJ and drop it in (usually I use GLB as ThreeJS seems to prefer that), in my case I just drop the URL into Cursor and let it figure out the placement of the asset into my game!

items.title:
BLF is "triply Bayesian":

1. The agent maintains a linguistic belief state (natural-language eviden...
items.content:
BLF is "triply Bayesian":

1. The agent maintains a linguistic belief state (natural-language evidence summaries paired with a probability), updated sequentially via tool calls (web search, data APIs).

2. Multi-trial outputs are aggregated using empirical Bayes (James-Stein-style shrinkage).

3. Calibrated via hierarchical Platt scaling.
items.author: @sirbayes
items.summary: BLF system described as 'triply Bayesian': linguistic belief state updated via tool calls, multi-trial empirical Bayes aggregation, and hierarchical Platt scaling calibration.
items.reasoning: Core technical description of a novel approach combining Bayesian principles with LLM agents. Good technical depth.
items.summary_html: BLF system described as 'triply Bayesian': linguistic belief state updated via tool calls, multi-trial empirical Bayes aggregation, and hierarchical Platt scaling calibration.
items.content_html:
BLF is "triply Bayesian":

1. The agent maintains a linguistic belief state (natural-language evidence summaries paired with a probability), updated sequentially via tool calls (web search, data APIs).

2. Multi-trial outputs are aggregated using empirical Bayes (James-Stein-style shrinkage).

3. Calibrated via hierarchical Platt scaling.

We used to pay $120,000/year for marketers to run ad campaigns.

Now, you can have an agent that connects to Meta, Google, TikTok, Shopify, LinkedIn, GA4, among others, then pulls your assets from Google Drive and turns them into live ad campaigns.

And it does this while letting you review/accept every change.

Things are changing faster than I can keep up.

items.title:
I got that little Home Assistant box https://t.co/q6afmz6k14

I can highly recommend Home Assistant ...
items.content:
I got that little Home Assistant box https://t.co/q6afmz6k14

I can highly recommend Home Assistant in general, I was recommended it by @johnonolan and @daniellockyer I remember

When I bought my house I hated home automation and didn't want any, cause I had so many bad experiences with some app you have to install in a hotel room or Airbnb and it always sucked so bad with some stupid iPad and there's no light swtiches, gladly F off

99% of home automation is just terrible

Home Assistant is open source and it's cool because everyone else with it makes plugins (integrations) to connect any device to it

Which solves the biggest problem in home automation, there's many different walled gardens: Amazon has Alexa, Apple has Homepod, Google has Google Home, and when you buy a smart device, it'll only work with a few of them or only one

That's where Home Assistant comes in, because it's open source, people hack all these devices to work on Home Assistant, so you can actually control EVERYTHING and I mean EVERYTHING

Our house came with a KNX system installed by the electricians, KNX is some more low level home automation system but also quite open, and last week he helped me export his KNX project file and add it my Home Assistant and it's great because everything still has regular light buttons

But now I can fix tiny annoyances in this house easily with AI talking to Home Assistant, like shut off the annoying toilet vent that people forget to switch off and just auto switch off after 15 min, or make the lights red at 10pm, or kill those ugly Portugal white LED spots permanently

Home automation is best when it gets into the background and just does things and doesn't annoy you like I said that 99% of home automation integrations in hotels and Airbnb do

It should be analog by default (analog switches) and then customizable on top of that

A dentist I interviewed a while back told me:

1. Sales went u...
items.content:
Small businesses will need our help.

A dentist I interviewed a while back told me:

1. Sales went up.
2. Customer satisfaction went up.
3. Employee morale went up.

After they automated their customer’s journey with AI (and their whole office). Only putting their fingers in your mouth isn’t automated. This is the best of breed pattern.

Spread the word. 

AI makes every business better. But they are busy doing other things. So need our help.

First you gotta convince them this is the way. That will prove difficult. But that is why money is money. If you could print it you would be the government. :-)

items.title: Backtesting LLM forecasters is hard — leakage from search results and/or training data  can silently...
items.content: Backtesting LLM forecasters is hard — leakage from search results and/or training data  can silently inflate results. We built a 4-layer date-leakage defense. A post-hoc LLM-powered audit finds leakage rate of at most 21/1375=1.5%. https://t.co/xhoiA6v1gf

@planet operates the largest cons...

items.summary_html: Planet (satellite company) using NVIDIA AI to process Earth observation satellite imagery 100-300x faster, enabling rapid disaster identification like wildfires.
items.content_html:
@UTAustin @UCSD @Livermore_Lab 🌌 AI applied to Earth observation.

@planet operates the largest constellation of Earth observation satellites. However, processing the data into workable images corrected for the physics and geometry of orbit may take hours.

Now, meaningful images can be obtained in seconds (sped up 100-300x — including decompression, orthorectification, and AI analysis) which can help identify wildfires and other disasters quickly.

🔗 https://t.co/6gXUxOVQxl

items.title:
Now, this is a crazy new assistant. 

@airjellyAI helps you remember, connect, and act on your perso...
items.content:
Now, this is a crazy new assistant. 

@airjellyAI helps you remember, connect, and act on your personal context across different apps. It can read your emails, look at your calendars, and even read X like you do.

How does it compare with other "computer" approaches? Well, it is proactive. 

Continually senses your context, already working before you say a word, and automatically closes the loop on your tasks. 

Others, like Chronicle/Codex are reactive. Memory only kicks in when you actively prompt them. I like AirJelly's approach better. Its AI continually senses your screen context and proactively builds its memory.

What AirJelly really does is work based on context memory. 

It remembers what you said in emails, what you added to your calendar, and what you’ve read or discussed on X. 

Then, when you need to take action later, it can recall those memories and help you move things forward.

Try it at https://t.co/2vBElfizyD.

Others, like Chronicle/Codex are reactive. Memory only kicks in when you actively prompt them. I like AirJelly's approach better. Its AI continually senses your screen context and proactively builds its memory.

What AirJelly really does is work based on context memory.

items.title: Today I hacked my Home Assistant with AI to nuke the bright white LED spots that came with my house ...
items.content:
Today I hacked my Home Assistant with AI to nuke the bright white LED spots that came with my house when I bought it

They're absolutely horrific to look at, they burn into your eyes, all new Portuguese houses come with them, I remember South Korean houses ALSO had these, it's like a factory light, I have no clue WHY anyone would want these

Luckily we have our own diffuse indirect light strips that just give off a glow and are much nicer

So I asked AI to redirect any signal that puts those shitty LED spots on, immediately KILL them and redirect them to my diffuse lights and it's wonderful

Also ALL my lights switch off at 10pm now and the ones that support color get turned on and switched to only RED light for sleep, so the entire house is red, very nice

items.title: Agents take ~4–5 tool calls per question on average, dominated by web search. But the meta-controlle...
items.content:
Agents take ~4–5 tool calls per question on average, dominated by web search. But the meta-controller routes source-specific tools intelligently — yfinance questions get time-series lookups, Polymarket questions get market queries, Wikipedia gets structured retrieval.

The meta-controller is distinct from the agent's policy (a prompted LLM). Learning it online via bandits is future work.
items.author: @sirbayes
items.summary: Details on BLF agent's meta-controller that routes source-specific tools intelligently based on question type (yfinance, Polymarket, Wikipedia).

The meta-controller is distinct from the agent's policy (a prompted LLM). Learning it online via bandits is future work.

items.title: I don't hate those who invent these dumb stories for hype. I know they are crooks, and they know the...
items.content:
I don't hate those who invent these dumb stories for hype. I know they are crooks, and they know they are crooks. If they do that, it means that the risks are expected to be lower than the business benefits, and they are ready to bear the consequences when the time will come. And it will come. Elizabeth Holmes is a witness.

I hate those leeches that spread these dumb stories by getting their thirty pieces of silver. Knowingly lying to people, making them anxious and angry, and for what? For a fucking ad click.

Pathetic pieces of shit.

items.content:
What if a single text prompt could generate an entire animated 3D scene?

Not a concept.

A real scene you can edit and export into production tools.

AI is starting to compress what used to be an entire VFX pipeline into one step.

I recently explored how this works with @autodesk.

And the implications for creative production are huge.

Let’s break it down.

items.title:
Most enterprises do not have an AI model problem.

They have an evaluation problem.

That was one of...
items.content:
Most enterprises do not have an AI model problem.

They have an evaluation problem.

That was one of my biggest takeaways from my conversation with @karibriski from Nvidia and @Toucas from Mistral AI at GTC.

In the agentic era, the winners will not be the companies running the most pilots.

They will be the ones that can measure what actually works, scale it, and turn it into revenue.

And the ecosystem is evolving fast:

→ Mistral AI announced Forge, enabling enterprises to build and customize their own models using their data and IP

→ Powered by NVIDIA’s accelerated infrastructure

→ NVIDIA also introduced the NEMO Tron coalition to build open models, datasets, and tools

Here’s the breakdown.

#NVIDIAPartner #NVIDIAGTC
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.reasoning: Main thread post with original GTC interview insights about enterprise AI evaluation challenges. Mentions specific product announcements (Mistral Forge, NEMO Tron). Though promotional, contains useful first-hand conference insights.

items.title:

I am setting the same complaints but when...
items.content:
@karatademada I am a big account, been here for 19 years.

I am setting the same complaints but when I check out their content it isn’t as good as what is showing up on my feed.

And I have the most complete lists of tech industry: https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ

And built my own algorithm to see the AI world here on X: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

The real problem is there is way too much content. My AI is reading 40,000 posts a day. No way any human can read that.

My feed is getting way better, which tells me the AI is picking better stuff and has less of a bias toward accounts with big followings than it used to.

Times are a changing. Only way to start relevant is to change with them.

items.title:
Looking through the new AI feed on my iPhone and first impressions are that it is very good. 

Very ...
items.content:
Looking through the new AI feed on my iPhone and first impressions are that it is very good. 

Very little noise. Good diversity of what it is showing.

Will help new people see what is going on in AI, which is really good.

Lists still have their role, especially in doing things with the X API and my AI news site, https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ which is wholly made from X lists, is a better way for someone who only has 10 minutes a day to keep up on all the important news.

I haven’t checked out the others yet, just the AI one, but it is really good. So I am happy.

What do you think?

items.title:
My takeaway is simple:

The next 2 to 3 years will reward companies that do 3 things well:

→ use AI...
items.content:
My takeaway is simple:

The next 2 to 3 years will reward companies that do 3 things well:

→ use AI internally, so leadership understands it firsthand
→ build with a clear path from pilot to industrialization
→ take control of their own models, data, and evaluation strategy

That is how AI stops being a demo and starts becoming a competitive moat.

And now the tools are here:

Mistral AI Forge makes model customization accessible, powered by NVIDIA infrastructure, while the NEMO Tron coalition is building an open ecosystem to democratize AI development.

Watch more NVIDIA GTC replays at https://t.co/atgeRhMsDH

items.title:
What do builders and leaders need to learn to thrive in the Agentforce transformation? #Sponsored

A...
items.content:
What do builders and leaders need to learn to thrive in the Agentforce transformation? #Sponsored

At @Salesforce TDX, Joe Inzerillo made a clear point: we’re not just adding AI; we’re onboarding a new kind of labor.

That changes everything about how we build software.
I used to design → build → test → deploy.
In the agentic world, you shape behavior, not just ship code.

In this conversation (streamed on @Salesforceplus1), we break down:
• Why agents aren’t traditional software
• The 4-layer enterprise AI architecture
• What this shift means for developers + CIOs

👉 Watch the full conversation: https://t.co/mxVeP9eDgh - https://t.co/Fs4X62pGai

Or check out our conversation on #CXOSpice  https://t.co/nvd5gbZdYx

#SalesforcePartner #Agentforce #Developers #TDX26

items.title:
Google is reading your email. Microsoft is reading your email. Apple is reading your email.

We all ...
items.content:
Google is reading your email. Microsoft is reading your email. Apple is reading your email.

We all let this happen to save a few bucks every month.

If the product is free, you are the product.

I use and pay for HEY. They respect my privacy.

There's only one downside: I really wish I had a way to run a local LLM on my HEY email.

This looks like a great experiment between Google Chrome and Atomic.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago (svpino) discusses email privacy concerns with major tech companies, promotes HEY email service, and mentions wanting local LLM integration for email. References a Google Chrome and Atomic experiment.

items.title:
The traditional workflow for a 3D scene looks something like this:

→ asset modeling
→ motion captur...
items.content:
The traditional workflow for a 3D scene looks something like this:

→ asset modeling
→ motion capture
→ camera tracking
→ animation passes
→ compositing layers

Each step adds time and complexity.

Now AI can generate editable 3D assets directly from:

→ text prompts
→ uploaded images

Which means creators start with the idea, not the pipeline.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.title: The second big insight, open and custom models are becoming a strategic advantage, not just a techni...
items.content:
The second big insight, open and custom models are becoming a strategic advantage, not just a technical preference.

Why?

→ More control over your AI stack
→ Less vendor lock-in
→ Better fit for regulated industries
→ More value from your own enterprise data and IP

General models are strong generalists.

But real differentiation comes when you make AI an expert in your domain, your workflows, your business.

That’s exactly where the market is heading:

With Mistral AI Forge, enterprises can build and fine-tune their own models, while NVIDIA’s NEMO Tron coalition is accelerating open innovation across models, datasets, and tooling.

items.title: @nikitabier Looking through the new AI feed on my iPhone and first impressions are that it is very g...
items.content:
@nikitabier Looking through the new AI feed on my iPhone and first impressions are that it is very good. 

Very little noise. Good diversity of what it is showing.

Will help new people see what is going on in AI, which is really good.

Lists still have their role, especially in doing things with the X API and my AI news site, https://t.co/W69DdrX22K which is wholly made from X lists, is a better way for someone who only has 10 minutes a day to keep up on all the important news.

I haven’t checked out the others yet, just the AI one, but it is really good. So I am happy.

items.title:
What surprised me most is how much of the technical pipeline can now be automated.

AI systems can h...
items.content:
What surprised me most is how much of the technical pipeline can now be automated.

AI systems can handle things like:

→ motion capture generation
→ camera tracking
→ alpha masks
→ character passes

The result is simple.

Less time preparing scenes.
More time iterating ideas.

items.title:
The shift happening right now is bigger than "which model should we use?"

AI is becoming a new way ...
items.content:
The shift happening right now is bigger than "which model should we use?"

AI is becoming a new way of building software.

That means leaders need to think in terms of:

→ test cases
→ evaluation frameworks
→ feedback loops
→ continuous optimization

If you cannot define success, you cannot move from POC to production.

That is why so many pilots stall.

At the same time, the foundation is changing:

Mistral AI’s Forge platform now allows enterprises to customize models end-to-end with their own data, while NVIDIA’s NEMO Tron coalition is pushing open AI development with shared models, datasets, and training tools.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon argues AI is becoming a new way of building software, requiring test cases, evaluation frameworks, and feedback loops. Promotes Mistral Forge and NVIDIA NEMO Tron.
items.reasoning: Valid point about AI evaluation challenges but low engagement, part of promotional thread

items.title: @karatademada Thanks! By the way, just had my AI do a new run for making https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ and...
items.content:
@karatademada Thanks! By the way, just had my AI do a new run for making https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ and it pulled almost 10,000 posts down from just my AI lists.

Which shows the problem. Too much content. And now the AI is much more focused on interests. 

My feed is much more interesting lately, so there's that. Even if reach is down.

But had one post get 40 million impressions over the weekend. You know who reshared one. 

My life didn't change. :-)

items.title: @BrianRoemmele @grok Looking through the new AI feed on my iPhone and first impressions are that it ...
items.content:
@BrianRoemmele @grok Looking through the new AI feed on my iPhone and first impressions are that it is very good. 

Very little noise. Good diversity of what it is showing.

Will help new people see what is going on in AI, which is really good.

Lists still have their role, especially in doing things with the X API and my AI news site, https://t.co/W69DdrX22K which is wholly made from X lists, is a better way for someone who only has 10 minutes a day to keep up on all the important news.

I haven’t checked out the others yet, just the AI one, but it is really good. So I am happy.

items.title: @chrisbest Looking through the new AI feed on my iPhone and first impressions are that it is very go...
items.content:
@chrisbest Looking through the new AI feed on my iPhone and first impressions are that it is very good. 

Very little noise. Good diversity of what it is showing.

Will help new people see what is going on in AI, which is really good.

Lists still have their role, especially in doing things with the X API and my AI news site, https://t.co/W69DdrX22K which is wholly made from X lists, is a better way for someone who only has 10 minutes a day to keep up on all the important news.

I haven’t checked out the others yet, just the AI one, but it is really good. So I am happy.

items.content:
🦧 AI helps primatologists protect critically endangered orangutans.

All three orangutan species are classified as critically endangered, with populations declining more than 80% over the past 75 years.

Conservationists are using NVIDIA-enabled deep learning, to train models that detect and count orangutan nests efficiently from aerial images, for more effective monitoring and management.

@itsumeshk I’m coming around the idea of using my phone for serious things.

items.content:
@itsumeshk I’m coming around the idea of using my phone for serious things.

I would never in a billion years write code from my phone, or mess with spreadsheets, or do anything I’d consider important.

But obviously, young people disagree with me, and there’s a huge push to make your phone your primary computing device capable of doing ANYTHING. 

I’m jealous.

items.summary: Santiago (@svpino) reflects on the generational shift toward phones as primary computing devices, noting younger users are increasingly doing serious work on mobile.
items.reasoning: Personal opinion tangentially related to computing trends, not AI-specific. Low engagement.
items.summary_html: Santiago (@svpino) reflects on the generational shift toward phones as primary computing devices, noting younger users are increasingly doing serious work on mobile.
items.content_html:
I’m coming around the idea of using my phone for serious things.

I would never in a billion years write code from my phone, or mess with spreadsheets, or do anything I’d consider important.

But obviously, young people disagree with me, and there’s a huge push to make your phone your primary computing device capable of doing ANYTHING.

I’m jealous.

items.title: Not too long ago a group of staff at a big 4 and an “AI ethics” person used to criticise my posts th...
items.content:
Not too long ago a group of staff at a big 4 and an “AI ethics” person used to criticise my posts that featured robotic images.saying robotics was too distant and remote and AI was mobile apps and web systems. 

They ignored how rapidly technology evolves and my intent to signal where AI was going to end up - that is into the physical world.

👉Enterprise CISO, look here! You can now get best-in-class Cyber Resilience on GCP (Google Cloud Platform). 👀

🌟Big news today from @Commvault at #GoogleCloudNext >> Commvault Extends the Full Breadth of its Next-Generation Cyber Resilience Platform to Google Cloud: https://t.co/VldJtVePja

💯By now offering top-tier cyber & identity resilience products and services on GCP, AWS Cloud, and Azure Cloud platforms, Commvault empowers enterprise CISOs to align and manage their resilience operations seamlessly on whichever Cloud Provider their applications already run.

🌐In conjunction with this, AI-powered Cyber Resilience platform #Commvault Cloud is now available through Google Cloud Marketplace, where partners and customers can accelerate time to value with simplified procurement, optimized costs, flexible access, and usage-based purchasing.

🏆Offerings include:

❇️ Resilience for modern workloads

♻️ Advanced security and threat hunting

❇️ Automated protection recommendations

♻️ Cloud data risk analysis

💎Testimonial: “In today’s AI era, business continuity depends on how quickly and confidently organizations can recover from disruption,” said Michael L. Matthews, VP of Global Learning & Innovation, Oral Roberts University. “Commvault’s collaboration with Google Cloud helps us simplify complexity, improve visibility across workloads, and strengthen cyber resilience to keep operations running.”

📊📈Visit Commvault at @GoogleCloud Next booth #3617: https://t.co/dFfij7rsJ9 (April 22-25, Las Vegas). Learn more. See demonstrations. Get expert insights. Sign up for an awesome giveaway. And more!

items.title: @mitgeniusz More like $30M-$50M for my companies but yes it's a sign of the style of company you wan...
items.content:
@mitgeniusz More like $30M-$50M for my companies but yes it's a sign of the style of company you want to build

If you raise VC, you have to go big to like $100M-$1B/year revenue, and if not you're shut down

If you're indie, you can do with $100K/year or $10M/year and it's fine and you own 100%

DocuSign is a success story, it has 3 founders who own about $250-$400M per person in stock

That's about 3-5x more than me depending how you count, so it's actually pretty close but I own 100%
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio discusses indie vs VC-funded business economics, comparing his ownership model to DocuSign founders' outcomes.
items.reasoning: Not AI-related. Business philosophy discussion about indie vs VC paths.
items.summary_html: Levelsio discusses indie vs VC-funded business economics, comparing his ownership model to DocuSign founders' outcomes.
items.content_html:
@mitgeniusz More like $30M-$50M for my companies but yes it's a sign of the style of company you want to build

If you raise VC, you have to go big to like $100M-$1B/year revenue, and if not you're shut down

If you're indie, you can do with $100K/year or $10M/year and it's fine and you own 100%

DocuSign is a success story, it has 3 founders who own about $250-$400M per person in stock

That's about 3-5x more than me depending how you count, so it's actually pretty close but I own 100%
